summary: Ritual invocation from Cajamarquilla (pre-Inca Wari site near Lima, ~600-1000 CE), held at AMNH New York. 8 cords, ALL STRING (100%), ALL LK (black). 0 INT, 0 EMPTY. Architecture 0-SEUL, depth L1, 1 cluster. 38% hit rate (low — compounds only partially glossed). Vocabulary is ESCALATING REDUPLICATION of a single root: qaqa (rock) -> qaqaqa (rock-TOPIC) -> qaqaqaqa (rock-rock, doubled) -> qaqaqaqaqa (rock-rock-TOPIC, TRIPLE). OKR cord notes reveal escalating A/B (S/Z) knot direction sequences: 3 knots -> 4 knots -> 5 knots. A ritual chant or toponym invocation — each cord adding one level to the rock's name.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  8 cords, depth L1 — minimal flat structure, single cluster.
  Architecture: 0-SEUL, 1 cluster.
  Colors: ALL LK (black) — 100% text/governance color.
  8 STRING (100%), 0 INT, 0 EMPTY.
  38% hit rate (low because compounds are partially glossed — the root qaqa is confirmed but multi-level reduplications exceed dictionary entries).
  Provenance: CAJAMARQUILLA — pre-Inca Wari site near Lima, occupation ~600-1000 CE.
  Museum: American Museum of Natural History (AMNH), New York.
  Associated: UR179-181 (the 'binary khipus' — pre-Inca, Z/U direction encoding).

COLOR-AS-ROLE
  LK (black, 8/8 cords = 100%): Text/governance color. Pure text — no numerical component, no color variation. The uniformity is itself meaningful: this is a single-purpose object.

ESCALATING REDUPLICATION
  qaqa x1 = rock (single, the root)
  qaqaqa x4 = qaqa+qa = rock-TOPIC (the rock as discourse topic)
  qaqaqaqa x2 = qaqa+qaqa = rock-rock (doubled root)
  qaqaqaqaqa x1 = qaqa+qaqa+qa = rock-rock-TOPIC (TRIPLE: doubled root + topic marker)

  The reduplication ESCALATES: 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 repetitions of the root syllable pair. Each cord adds one level to the name of the rock.

KNOT DIRECTION PATTERNS (from OKR)
  C1: B, A, A (3 knots)
  C2: A, B, B, A (4 knots)
  C3: A, B, A, B, A (5 knots)
  The knot count ESCALATES: 3 -> 4 -> 5. And the A/B (= S/Z twist direction) patterns grow progressively more complex: BAA -> ABBA -> ABABA.
  The escalation is not just lexical (more syllables) but PHYSICAL (more knots, more complex direction patterns).

CROSS-CORPUS
  UR181 belongs to the UR179-181 group — the 'binary khipus' from Cajamarquilla, all pre-Inca, all using Z/U direction encoding. UR120 (Huacho) shows similar reduplication patterns but less extreme. UR181 is the most extreme reduplication in the entire corpus — the shortest text (8 cords) with the deepest lexical recursion.

NARRATIVE
  Rock. Rock-rock. Rock-rock-rock. Rock-rock-rock-rock. That is the entire text — eight cords of escalating invocation, each one adding another syllable to the name of a sacred stone. Qaqa on the first cord, qaqaqa on the next four, qaqaqaqa doubled, and finally qaqaqaqaqa — the rock named five syllables deep, its name growing with each cord like a chant building toward ecstasy.

  All eight cords are black — the color of governance and text — and each one carries a progressively longer sequence of A and B knot directions: three knots, then four, then five. The escalation is not just lexical but physical, encoded in the twist of the fibers themselves.

  At Cajamarquilla, the ancient Wari site near Lima where occupation spans from roughly 600 to 1000 CE, this eight-cord object may be the shortest ritual text in the corpus — an invocation of a rock, or a toponym that required five levels of naming to capture the full sacred significance of a single stone. The Wari preceded the Inca by centuries, and their khipus — the 'binary khipus' of the UR179-181 group — encode information differently: not in knot values but in knot directions, S and Z, A and B, building patterns that grow more elaborate with each cord. This is not accounting. This is naming. And the name grows.
references: Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Ritual invocation analysis, escalating reduplication
Cajamarquilla archaeological site — pre-Inca Wari, ~600-1000 CE, near Lima
Cross-corpus: UR179-181 ('binary khipus' — pre-Inca Z/U direction encoding)
Cross-corpus: UR120 (Huacho — similar reduplication, less extreme)
Urton, G. (2003). Signs of the Inka Khipu — binary coding and knot direction
